translation: By the sky containing great stars
commentary:
وَٱلسَّمَآءِ ذَاتِ ٱلْبُرُوجِ

The surah opens with a powerful oath: "I swear by the sky that possesses Al-Buruj." This term, commonly translated as "constellations" or "towers," carries layers of meaning that illuminate the deeper message of this surah.

The word buruj refers to fortified towers or castle battlements—the imposing guard towers from which sentries watch and defend. But the root of this word also appears in contexts describing beauty and prominence. When the Quran uses the verb form in *watatabarrajna tabarruja al-jahiliyyah*, it speaks of women displaying their adornment. Similarly, *tabarruj* describes flowers in full bloom, their most striking parts capturing all attention. The word can even refer to the space between beautifully arched eyebrows or the captivating color of someone's eyes. The common thread is something that stands out, something spectacular that commands attention.

Allah ﷻ is drawing our focus to two essential qualities of the sky: its breathtaking beauty—those endless constellations visible in the darkness of night—and its protective nature, like fortresses securing what lies within.

This isn't merely a scientific statement about astronomy. It's a spiritual reality. Throughout the Quran, we learn about seven heavens, each with checkpoints where angels guard and grant passage to souls ascending through the levels. When jinn attempted to steal information from heavenly conversations, they were struck down by meteors—*shahaban thaaqiban*. In Surah Al-Jinn, we learn that when the Quran began its descent, the jinn found the skies suddenly inaccessible. Security had intensified. They wondered: *Has some evil been intended for the earth, or does their Lord intend for them something good?*

Only when they encountered the Prophet ﷺ reciting the Quran did they understand. The skies were locked down because Jibril (AS) was descending with divine revelation. Each of these towers—these *buruj*—bears witness to that sacred journey of revelation coming down, and to the parallel journey of our souls and deeds ascending.

When Allah ﷻ swears by *as-sama'i dhati al-buruj*, He connects us to this spiritual reality: revelation descends from these fortified checkpoints, while our prayers, our deeds, and the souls of the departed ascend through them.

translation: And [by] the promised Day
commentary:
وَٱلْيَوْمِ ٱلْمَوْعُودِ

After swearing by the fortified sky, Allah ﷻ swears by *al-yawm al-maw'ud*—the Day that has been promised. This is the Day of Judgment, and the progression here is significant.

Towers and fortresses represent defense—keeping enemies out, maintaining security. But the Day of Judgment is not defensive; it's the day of divine action. Those angelic armies currently stationed in the heavens will descend in rows upon rows—*saffan saffan*. The stars you see holding their positions will transform from guards to an advancing force.

There's another crucial dimension here. Throughout history, nations given divine guidance eventually lost the pure teachings about Allah ﷻ—His names, His qualities, His oneness, and the relationship we should have with Him. Similarly, teachings about the Akhirah became corrupted, diluted, confused with cultural additions and ignorance. Even among communities that claim to possess revelation today, authentic knowledge of the promised Day has largely disappeared.

By linking these two oaths—the fortified sky through which the Quran descended, and the Day that has been promised—Allah ﷻ establishes that this final revelation came through such secure channels that the truth about that Day will never disappear so long as the Quran remains among us.

The term *al-maw'ud* is particularly significant. It's not merely that the Day will come, but that its details are well-defined and well-known. Through this Quran, the reality of that Day—how judgment will be passed, what criteria Allah ﷻ will use—remains crystal clear and protected.

translation: And [by] the witness and what is witnessed
commentary:
وَشَاهِدٍ وَمَشْهُودٍ

Two small words: "By the witness and the witnessed." Yet they contain profound realities that connect everything in this surah.

Consider the progression: The fortified towers in the sky serve as watchtowers, and what is the purpose of a watchtower? To witness. The guards stationed there observe everything below. The stars themselves are witnesses (*shahid*) to our deeds, and we are the ones being witnessed (*mashhud*).

The Day that has been promised? That is the ultimate *mashhud*—the day that shall be witnessed by all creation.

There's a hadith, considered weak in chain but mentioned by scholars for its meanings, that identifies *ash-shahid* as Friday and *al-mashhud* as the Day of Arafah. The distinction makes sense: we witness Friday wherever we are, but Arafah requires us to go there—the place itself must be witnessed for the pilgrimage to be complete.

But these two words capture so much more. Allah ﷻ Himself declares: *Shahida Allahu annahu la ilaha illa Huwa*—Allah witnesses that there is no deity except Him. He is the ultimate Witness, making all of us the witnessed. The Quran states: *Wa kafaa billahi shahida*—Sufficient is Allah as Witness.

On Judgment Day, the Messenger ﷺ will stand as witness over this Ummah, testifying about us. Every nation will come with their messenger to testify. Angels assigned to record our deeds will be witnesses—*kiraman katibin*—noble scribes who write down everything they witness.

Even our own bodies will testify. *Wa qalu li-juludihim lima shahidtum 'alayna*—people will ask their very skin: "Why did you testify against us?" Our eyes, hands, feet, and tongues will all bear witness to what we did. The disbelievers will witness both Paradise and Hellfire. *Burrizat al-jahimu liman yara*—Hell will be blazed for all to see. *Uzlifat al-jannatu lil-muttaqin*—Paradise will be brought close for the righteous to witness.

In this surah specifically, there's another layer: disbelievers who tortured Muslims will themselves be witnesses to their own crimes. They won't need anyone else to testify against them—they themselves will be *shahid* to what they did, and the fire awaiting them will be their *mashhud*.

Remember when the disciples of Isa (AS) faced persecution and asked their prophet: *Fakun ma'a ash-shahidin*—be among those who bear witness. Be our witness that we were Muslims. They wanted that testimony secured before they were killed. This echoes the instruction we give ourselves in every prayer: *Ash-hadu an la ilaha illallah*—I testify. We constantly remind ourselves to be among the witnesses.

At the Prophet's ﷺ final public address, he asked: "Have I conveyed the message?" The Ummah responded that he had. He then declared: *Allahumma fashhad, Allahumma fashhad, Allahumma fashhad*—O Allah, bear witness! From that moment, the entire Ummah was charged with being witnesses (*shahid*) over humanity, while the Messenger ﷺ is witness over us.

## Ayah 4-5

قُتِلَ أَصْحَـٰبُ ٱلْأُخْدُودِ
ٱلنَّارِ ذَاتِ ٱلْوَقُودِ

After establishing the framework of witnessing—that the skies bear witness, the Day of Judgment is coming, and nothing goes unnoticed—Allah ﷻ declares: *Qutila as-habu al-ukhdud. An-nari dhati al-waqud.*

This can be translated two ways: "The people of the trench were killed," or "Cursed be the people of the trench"—a prayer for their destruction. The word *ukhdud* refers to a deep ditch or trench. Interestingly, the same root describes the cavity of an open mouth, and here it refers to massive ditches dug into the earth.

There's a detailed hadith in Sahih Muslim narrated by Suhayb Ar-Rumi (RA) about a king who had an elderly sorcerer. The sorcerer requested an apprentice to train, and a young brilliant boy was assigned to him. On his way to lessons, the boy would pass by a monk who taught him about Allah ﷻ. The boy's iman grew so strong that he stopped practicing magic entirely and began making dua for people. Allah ﷻ granted him miracles—people were healed through his supplications.

When asked how he healed anyone, he would simply say: "I don't heal anyone. Allah ﷻ heals." This reached the king, who was enraged: "You mean I'm not your master? Someone else heals?" The old monk was tortured and killed. They tried multiple times to kill the boy—throwing him off a cliff, drowning him at sea—but he survived each attempt.

Finally, the boy told the king: "If you really want to kill me, take an arrow, point it at my head, and say: 'In the name of the Master of this boy, the Rabb of this boy'—essentially *Bismillah ar-Rahman ar-Rahim*—and shoot me. Then I will die." The king gathered all the people, shot him through the head with that declaration, and the boy died. Immediately, the entire crowd declared: "We believe in the Master of this boy!" The entire population embraced Islam.

Now furious, the king had massive trenches dug and ordered that all these new believers be thrown into fire-filled ditches and burned alive. This is literally *as-habu al-ukhdud*—the people of the ditch.

However, there's scholarly discussion about whether this hadith necessarily refers to this specific ayah. Some classical scholars note that this is Makkan Quran, where the primary audience consisted of non-Muslims who heard Allah's words directly without accompanying explanation. The Quran had to be self-evident to them—*qur'anan 'arabiyyan mubinan*, clear Arabic speech.

From that perspective, people thrown into massive ditches to be burned in fire with great fuel sounds remarkably similar to descriptions of Jahannam found throughout the Quran. This has led to two sets of interpretations: one viewing this as a historical account of people tortured and burned (perhaps generalizing about all such genocidal persecution), and another viewing this as describing disbelievers who will ultimately end up in those ditches of eternal fire.

The fuel (*waqud*) of these fires? We've learned elsewhere in the Quran that people themselves become the fuel of Hell's fire.

## Ayah 6-7

إِذْ هُمْ عَلَيْهَا قُعُودٌ
وَهُمْ عَلَىٰ مَا يَفْعَلُونَ بِٱلْمُؤْمِنِينَ شُهُودٌ

*Idh hum 'alayha qu'ud*—as they sit upon it. Some scholars interpret this as the perpetrators sitting at the very edge of the fire before being thrown in, already destroyed even before entering. Others see it as people piled on top of the flames. Either way, there's a consistent Quranic theme of describing not just what happens after you're in your grave, but the moments right before being thrown in.

*Wa hum 'ala ma yaf'aluna bil-mu'minina shuhud*—and they are witnesses over what they used to do to the believers. Notice the word *shuhud* appears again, connecting back to *wa shahidin wa mashhud*. These are people who committed atrocities and tortured believers. Now, as they're about to be thrown into fire, they themselves bear full witness to their crimes. No court needed, no external testimony required—they themselves testify to what they did.

Here, they become the *shahid* (witness), and the fire they're about to see becomes the *mashhud* (the witnessed). This creates a beautiful internal coherence within the surah.

translation: Cursed were the companions of the trench
[Containing] the fire full of fuel
When they were sitting near it
And they, to what they were doing against the believers, were witnesses
And they resented them not except because they believed in Allah, the Exalted in Might, the Praiseworthy
commentary:
وَمَا نَقَمُوا۟ مِنْهُمْ إِلَّآ أَن يُؤْمِنُوا۟ بِٱللَّهِ ٱلْعَزِيزِ ٱلْحَمِيدِ

*Wa ma naqamu minhum illa an yu'minu billahi al-'Aziz al-Hamid.* The word *naqama* is unique in the Quran—not commonly used. It doesn't simply mean hatred; it describes a hatred so intense that it changes the color of your eyes. When someone despises something so thoroughly that they're consumed with disgust, when they refuse to hear any argument in someone's defense—that's *niqmah*.

Allah ﷻ asks: What made them hate the believers with such burning intensity? What level of hatred enables human beings to commit such inhumane acts—torture, burning, killing? The answer is devastating in its simplicity: *Illa an yu'minu billahi*—except that they believed in Allah ﷻ. That's it. Their only crime was faith.

But notice Allah ﷻ doesn't stop at "they believed in Allah." He adds two of His names: *Al-'Aziz Al-Hamid*—The Mighty, The Praiseworthy.

Why these specific names here? These criminals committing these atrocities have no recognition of Allah's authority. They commit their crimes with impunity, thinking no one will ever hold them accountable. If they truly recognized authority, they wouldn't act this way—a criminal who sees police lights drops his weapon and runs.

Allah ﷻ is *Al-'Aziz*—The Absolute Authority—whether they recognize it or not. Whether they think He's watching or not, His authority remains undiminished. But this ayah also carries profound consolation for believers who are victims: Allah ﷻ remains the Authority. While they torture you, they gain nothing and you lose nothing of ultimate value. A day will come when none of your losses were truly lost and none of their gains were truly gained. Allah ﷻ has the authority to make everything right.

And *Al-Hamid*—The Praiseworthy. This name is particularly remarkable here. Not *Al-Mahmud* (the One who is praised), but *Al-Hamid*—He who is inherently worthy of all praise, regardless of whether anyone praises Him. He doesn't need our praise. Whether you praise Him or not changes nothing about Him; withholding praise only results in your own loss.

For believers facing persecution, this is when Shaytan whispers: "What kind of God didn't save you? Where was He when this happened to your family, your home?" This is when faith in Allah's names—*Al-'Aziz* and *Al-Hamid*—is most severely tested. The ayah reinforces these exact names at the moment when belief in them is most challenged.

translation: To whom belongs the dominion of the heavens and the earth. And Allah, over all things, is Witness
commentary:
ٱلَّذِى لَهُۥ مُلْكُ ٱلسَّمَـٰوَٰتِ وَٱلْأَرْضِ ۚ وَٱللَّهُ عَلَىٰ كُلِّ شَىْءٍ شَهِيدٌ

Allah ﷻ goes further: *Alladhi lahu mulku as-samawati wal-ard*—He to whom belongs the dominion of the heavens and the earth. The dominion of the heavens already came up—what's in the skies that alludes to His kingdom? The fortresses, the towers. 

And it's not just in the skies—it's on the earth too. Just because you haven't seen earthly consequences yet, don't think you have free reign. The difference between the skies and earth right now is simply this: you're under surveillance. Your crimes are being watched and recorded. You won't be caught immediately, but you're certainly being monitored.

*WAllahu 'ala kulli shay'in shahid*—and Allah ﷻ is Witness over all things. This is *wa shahidin wa mashhud* reaching its climax. What greater witness exists than Allah ﷻ? The stars can witness, angels can witness, victims can witness—but no witness compares to Allah ﷻ Himself. He has always been, and remains, a constant Witness over every single thing.

Never think for a moment that Allah ﷻ didn't watch what happened.

translation: Indeed, those who have tortured the believing men and believing women and then have not repented will have the punishment of Hell, and they will have the punishment of the Burning Fire
commentary:
إِنَّ ٱلَّذِينَ فَتَنُوا۟ ٱلْمُؤْمِنِينَ وَٱلْمُؤْمِنَـٰتِ ثُمَّ لَمْ يَتُوبُوا۟ فَلَهُمْ عَذَابُ جَهَنَّمَ وَلَهُمْ عَذَابُ ٱلْحَرِيقِ

*Inna alladhina fatanu al-mu'minina wal-mu'minat*—No doubt those who put believing men and believing women to trial. Notice Allah ﷻ specifically mentions *mu'minin* and *mu'minat*—believing men and believing women. This highlights that trials and persecution affect men and women differently, and this acknowledgment extends to the worst case: physical torture.

But more importantly, Allah ﷻ doesn't say *alladhina kafaru* (those who disbelieved). He says *alladhina fatanu*—anyone who put believers to trial. This isn't limited to disbelievers persecuting Muslims. It includes Muslims hurting other Muslims.

As much as Muslim blood is being spilled worldwide, we must ask the hard question: how much of it is being spilled by fellow Muslims? How much slander, accusation, humiliation, theft, and cheating against Muslims comes from other Muslims? Sometimes our treatment of each other makes us wish for the manners of Quraysh.

The word *fatanu* also carries beautiful embedded meaning. Originally, *fitnah* describes the process of testing gold's purity by melting it at extremely high temperatures. All impurities rise and separate, revealing the gold's true worth. When believers are put through difficulty, torture, and painful situations, they're being purified. To Allah ﷻ, believers are like gold—valuable. The persecution serves as purification, removing sins, strengthening reliance on Him, deepening faith.

*Thumma lam yatubu*—and after doing all that, they didn't make tawbah. Notice: to purify metal, you must melt it with fire. The perpetrators put believers through fire, and now Allah ﷻ will put them through fire: *fa-lahum 'adhabu jahannama wa lahum 'adhabu al-hariq*—they shall have the punishment of Jahannam and the punishment of the burning fire.

Jahannam (Gehinnom in Hebrew) originally meant a valley of trash, though for us it carries far graver significance. And just to be absolutely clear what Jahannam entails, Allah ﷻ adds: *wa lahum 'adhabu al-hariq*—the punishment of endless, consuming fire.

It's worth noting that Ibn Rajab (may Allah have mercy on him) connected this to a historical event in Yemen where a Jewish empire captured Christian towns and forced conversion to Judaism, burning alive those who refused. Whether or not this specific interpretation is correct, it's interesting that the Jews didn't believe in Hell the way we do. They believed in Jahannam, but not as a place of eternal burning—yet here Allah ﷻ says *'adhabu jahannama wa 'adhabu al-hariq*.

translation: Indeed, those who have believed and done righteous deeds will have gardens beneath which rivers flow. That is the great attainment
commentary:
إِنَّ ٱلَّذِينَ ءَامَنُوا۟ وَعَمِلُوا۟ ٱلصَّـٰلِحَـٰتِ لَهُمْ جَنَّـٰتٌ تَجْرِى مِن تَحْتِهَا ٱلْأَنْهَـٰرُ ۚ ذَٰلِكَ ٱلْفَوْزُ ٱلْكَبِيرُ

The flip side: *Inna alladhina amanu wa 'amilu as-salihat*—no doubt those who believed and continued doing righteous deeds. Notice the beautiful contrast: fire on one side, rivers of water on the other.

*Lahum jannatin tajri min tahtiha al-anhar*—for them are gardens beneath which rivers flow. *Dhalika al-fawzu al-kabir*—that truly is the ultimate success.

The word *fawz* in Arabic means achieving something after great toil and labor. After all the persecution, torture, and trials believers endured—that patience, that steadfastness—leads to *al-fawz al-kabir*, the ultimate success. There is no greater success than this.

translation: Indeed, the vengeance of your Lord is severe
commentary:
إِنَّ بَطْشَ رَبِّكَ لَشَدِيدٌ

Having promised Paradise, Allah ﷻ returns to the original theme of not letting criminals escape. He's been watching, but watching without immediately catching. When someone witnesses a crime and does nothing despite having power to intervene, we get upset: "There's a police officer right there! Why didn't you do something?"

The same question could be asked of Allah ﷻ: Don't You have the power to change this? Don't You have the power to intervene?

*Inna batsha rabbika la-shadid*—No doubt about it, the crushing grip of your Master is truly severe. The word *batsh* describes tightening your grip on something so much that it's about to break—like crushing an egg in your hand until it collapses. Allah ﷻ is saying: if I were to grab, nothing would survive My grip.

Notice He addresses His messenger directly: *rabbika*—your Rabb. This is a hint that the Prophet ﷺ himself was being tortured, his Companions were being tortured, and some wondered when Allah's help would arrive. The answer comes through this ayah.

translation: Indeed, it is He who originates [creation] and repeats
commentary:
إِنَّهُۥ هُوَ يُبْدِئُ وَيُعِيدُ

*Innahu huwa yubdi'u wa yu'id*—He is the One who starts things and brings them back. Allah ﷻ can punish in this life and in the next life. He can start punishment now and bring it back again later.

Hasn't He done this with many nations? He punished the people of 'Ad in this dunya and will punish them again in the Akhirah. He started their punishment here and will bring it back. 

*Yubdi'u wa yu'id* also means He creates life and brings life back again. Don't wonder whether the next life is truly coming. And this isn't just about believing in the afterlife—it's about believing that in the next life, justice will be served. That's the point. Just as these events are happening now, Allah ﷻ will bring all these people back to account for every action.

translation: And He is the Forgiving, the Affectionate
commentary:
وَهُوَ ٱلْغَفُورُ ٱلْوَدُودُ

*Wa huwa al-Ghafur al-Wadud*—and He is the Extremely Forgiving, the Intensely Loving.

Everyone knows *Al-Ghafur*, but let's explore *Al-Wadud*. The word describes holding onto something gently and softly, never letting go. It's love that doesn't rust, doesn't die out, doesn't fade—it stays and stays and stays. *Wudd* is long-lasting love. Interestingly, pre-Islamic pagan traditions used *Wudd* as the name of their god of love (similar to Cupid in later traditions).

Here we find that Allah ﷻ loves His servants and extends His love, care, and mercy to them. His care lasts forever.

There are two audiences here. First, believers who have been persecuted: know that this might be a means by which you'll be forgiven, and beyond that, Allah ﷻ will shower you with love and care that lasts forever.

Second, even the criminals are being told: make tawbah now. Allah ﷻ will forgive you. You still have a chance while you're alive. Make tawbah and your record will be expunged. You will be forgiven.

translation: Honorable Owner of the Throne
commentary:
ذُو ٱلْعَرْشِ ٱلْمَجِيدُ

*Dhu al-'Arsh al-Majid*—Owner of the Glorious Throne. We think of the fortresses in the sky, those towers described at the beginning. Far above all of them is the greatest structure: the Throne of Allah ﷻ itself. He is the owner of *al-'Arsh al-Majid*.

Let's understand *Majid*. We use it constantly—*Al-Qur'an al-Majid*—but what does it mean? In Arabic, *majd* describes being perfectly filled with nutritious, wholesome food. An animal in perfect health that's eaten exactly the right food has *majd*. From this developed the meaning of nobility, dignity, generosity, and goodness toward others.

When the best of all qualities combine in something—noble, respectable, likable, generous—that's *majid*. It's the perfection of qualities.

When you think of a king's throne, you often think of oppression, power, injustice stemming from that seat. Yet Allah ﷻ describes His Throne as *Majid*—possessing all the good qualities associated with nobility: justice, generosity, goodness toward people. That's what's associated with Allah's Throne.

translation: Effecter of what He intends
commentary:
فَعَّالٌ لِّمَا يُرِيدُ

*Fa''alun lima yurid*—Doer over and over again of whatever He wills. This ayah shouldn't be looked at in isolation; there's continuity here.

He just described that the Throne from which He rules is a throne possessing *majd*—goodness, nobility. From that throne of nobility and justice, He does whatever He wills. You might think "He does whatever He wants" means He could be unjust if He wanted, or let persecution happen if He wanted. But He already established that His Throne has *majd* in it—goodness and nobility.

Therefore, *fa''alun lima yurid* means only good things. Whatever He intends can only be good, and He does it over and over and over again. All His actions stem from that throne of perfect justice and nobility.

## Ayah 17-18

هَلْ أَتَىٰكَ حَدِيثُ ٱلْجُنُودِ
فِرْعَوْنَ وَثَمُودَ

*Hal ataka hadithu al-junud. Fir'awna wa Thamud*—Has the news of the armies reached you? The armies of Fir'awn and Thamud.

This statement is striking. Is Allah ﷻ telling the Prophet ﷺ about reinforcements coming? No—historical armies. What were Fir'awn and Thamud? They were armies. They possessed such military might. Yet how did Allah ﷻ deal with them?

You think He can't deal with these criminals? He's dealt with far greater military powers before. He's dealt with nations far more skilled at oppression, torture, and persecution. The message is clear: these current oppressors are nothing compared to what He's already destroyed.

translation: Has there reached you the story of the soldiers
[Those of] Pharaoh and Thamud
But they who disbelieve are in [persistent] denial
commentary:
بَلِ ٱلَّذِينَ كَفَرُوا۟ فِى تَكْذِيبٍ

*Bal alladhina kafaru fi takdhib*—Instead, these disbelievers are just in outright denial. They're going to call all of that a lie. They don't realize how much history has passed, how many more powerful than them have been dealt with. They can deny it all they want. They can dismiss it.

But don't forget...

translation: While Allah encompasses them from behind
commentary:
وَٱللَّهُ مِن وَرَآئِهِم مُّحِيطٌۢ

*WAllahu min wara'ihim muhit*—Allah ﷻ, in fact, has been behind them, encircling them entirely. This imagery is beautiful following the mention of armies. When an army attacks a village, it surrounds it. An army positions itself strategically, ensuring it cannot be attacked from behind.

Allah ﷻ says He has come from behind them and encircled them entirely. These armies thought they had their victims surrounded, but Allah ﷻ has them surrounded from behind. That's the image painted here.

translation: But this is an honored Qur'an
commentary:
بَلْ هُوَ قُرْءَانٌ مَّجِيدٌ

What is this great power that has them surrounded? It's not a physical weapon. It's not even angels.

*Bal huwa qur'anun majid*—No, in fact, it is a Glorious Quran. Allah ﷻ could destroy them, but instead, He gave them the Quran. The Quran is noble and generous. Allah ﷻ won't immediately punish them because of the nobility of the Quran itself. He's giving them a chance to straighten themselves out through the Quran's majesty.

Instead of Allah ﷻ destroying nations by His hand—which He could—He gives them opportunity through His generous Book. Had it not been for the Quran, nations would have been wiped out. But no, they still have a chance. Perhaps they'll return to the Quran, and through its majesty and grace, they themselves will become dignified.

Notice the Throne is *Majid*, and now the Quran is *Majid*. The recitation that gives nobility is coming from the Throne that is noble. We should internalize this: no matter how much people ridicule the Quran, its grace and majesty should never diminish in our eyes—not in how we speak of it, not in how we recite it.

The fact that we have something sharing qualities with Allah's Throne connects us directly to *al-'Arsh al-Majid*. These words carry royalty. We must be in awe of the Word of Allah ﷻ that came from *Dhu al-'Arsh al-Majid*.

translation: [Inscribed] in a Preserved Slate
commentary:
فِى لَوْحٍ مَّحْفُوظٍۭ

*Fi lawhin mahfudh*—in a Preserved Tablet. The word *lawh* in Arabic means a board, a flat wooden surface you can write or carve on. Allah ﷻ says the Quran lies in *lawh mahfudh*—a guarded, protected, preserved tablet.

This is crucial to understand: the origin of this Quran we recite isn't the mouth of the Prophet ﷺ. The origin isn't even Jibril (AS). Jibril (AS) receives it from *Al-Lawh Al-Mahfudh*—a tablet well-guarded and secured in Allah's possession. From that guarded tablet, the Quran was delivered to the heart and tongue of Rasulullah ﷺ, and through him to all of us.

We are now connected to this heavenly fortress, this heavenly security. People sometimes say, "You better watch out—I've got connections." When someone has a connection with the Quran, they truly have something to say. They've admitted into their heart that they carry *Qur'an Majid* coursing through their veins—words from the Preserved Tablet, from the Glorious Throne, from the Owner of all nobility and justice.

May Allah ﷻ make us people of the Quran.
